45.problem_description: Sum all integers in an array, but exclude any contiguous sequences that start with 6 and end with 7 (inclusive). Return the computed sum.

kcs.name: Array traversal with index-based loop
kcs.reasoning: All solutions iterate through the array using a for loop with index counter and array.length boundary.

kcs.name: State machine with boolean flag
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 1, 3, and 4 use a boolean flag to toggle between 'in skip mode' and 'normal mode' when encountering 6 and 7.

kcs.name: Nested loop for forward skipping
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 2 and 5 use an inner while loop to advance the array index past the closing 7 when a 6 is encountered.

kcs.name: Conditional accumulation in loops
kcs.reasoning: All solutions selectively add array elements to sum only when outside a 6-7 block, combining loop logic with aggregation.

kcs.name: Loop counter modification in body
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 2 and 5 modify the for loop's index variable inside an inner while loop, requiring careful index boundary management.

kcs.name: Empty or edge case handling
kcs.reasoning: Solution 5 explicitly checks array length; others rely on loop condition to handle empty arrays safely.

48.problem_description: Remove all instances of the value 10 from an array, compact non-10 elements to the front, and fill remaining positions with zeros while preserving array length.

kcs.name: Conditional filtering within array iteration
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use if statements to test each element (nums[i] != 10 or nums[i] == 10) and selectively process only qualifying elements.

kcs.name: Two-pointer index tracking technique
kcs.reasoning: Solutions maintain separate indices: one for iterating the input (i/j) and one for writing filtered output (place/x/j/c), enabling compact placement without array shift overhead.

kcs.name: Array creation and initialization
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 2-5 explicitly create new arrays; Solution 1 reuses the input array; all demonstrate instantiating arrays with fixed size and accessing array object references.

kcs.name: Padding array remainder with default values
kcs.reasoning: All solutions fill positions from the last filtered element to array.length with 0 using a second loop, maintaining correct array length.

kcs.name: Multiple sequential loop execution
kcs.reasoning: Each solution uses at least two separate loops: one to filter and compact elements, and another to zero-pad the remainder, requiring careful loop sequencing.

kcs.name: Method return of modified array reference
kcs.reasoning: All solutions return an int[] reference (either the modified input or a newly created array), demonstrating non-void return types for arrays.

51.problem_description: Modify an array so that after each number divisible by 10, all subsequent non-multiples of 10 are replaced with that divisible-by-10 value, until another multiple of 10 is encountered.

kcs.name: Modulo operator for divisibility testing
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use % 10 == 0 or % 10 != 0 to identify multiples of 10, which is the primary condition controlling the replacement logic.

kcs.name: State tracking across loop iterations
kcs.reasoning: Solutions maintain a variable (e.g., bhag, runNum, val, ten) that remembers the most recent multiple of 10 encountered, enabling replacements in subsequent iterations.

kcs.name: Conditional element replacement in arrays
kcs.reasoning: Each solution uses if/else conditions to decide whether to replace the current element based on both the element value and tracked state.

kcs.name: Array in-place or copy mutation
kcs.reasoning: Solutions modify array elements via index assignment (nums[i] = value) either directly on the input array or on a copy.

kcs.name: Loop control with array bounds
kcs.reasoning: Solutions iterate through the entire array using i < nums.length or similar bounds, with some using nested loops to skip past multiples of 10.

kcs.name: Non-void array return from method
kcs.reasoning: All solutions return the modified array, requiring correct method signature and return statement with array type.

56.problem_description: Calculate the average of array elements after removing the single largest and smallest values using integer division.

kcs.name: Array traversal with single/multiple loops
kcs.reasoning: All solutions iterate through the array; Solution 5 uses two separate loops while others use one, demonstrating different traversal strategies.

kcs.name: Min/max value tracking with conditionals
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 1-4 use if/else comparisons or Math.min/max to maintain running minimum and maximum; Solution 5 additionally tracks index positions.

kcs.name: Accumulator pattern for summation
kcs.reasoning: All solutions maintain a running sum variable incremented by each array element throughout traversal.

kcs.name: Initialize min/max with first element
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 1-4 initialize min and max with nums[0] before looping to avoid incorrect comparisons with default values.

kcs.name: Integer division for average computation
kcs.reasoning: All solutions divide the adjusted sum by (length - 2) using integer division, which truncates toward zero.

kcs.name: Subtract extremes from sum algebraically
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 1-4 compute average as (sum - min - max) / (length - 2), while Solution 5 filters elements by position instead.

57.problem_description: Create and populate an integer array of size n with sequential values from 0 to n-1, then return the array.

kcs.name: Array instantiation with size parameter
kcs.reasoning: All solutions declare and initialize an array using new int[n], establishing the array's fixed size.

kcs.name: Index-based loop iteration with bounds
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use for loops with i < n (or i < list.length) to iterate through each array index safely.

kcs.name: Array element assignment by index
kcs.reasoning: Each solution assigns a value to array[i], demonstrating indexed access for element modification.

kcs.name: Loop counter as sequential value
kcs.reasoning: Solutions leverage the loop variable i or an equivalent counter to generate and assign incrementing values 0 to n-1.

kcs.name: Array return from method
kcs.reasoning: The method signature returns int[] and all solutions properly return the populated array, requiring non-void array return type understanding.

64.problem_description: Generate a String array for a range [start, end) where each element is 'Fizz' (divisible by 3), 'Buzz' (divisible by 5), 'FizzBuzz' (divisible by 15), or the number as a string.

kcs.name: Array instantiation with computed size
kcs.reasoning: All solutions create a String array with size (end - start) to store results for the given range.

kcs.name: Loop index to array offset mapping
kcs.reasoning: Solutions map loop variables to actual values using either (i - start) as index or (i + start) for divisibility checks, demonstrating index offset techniques.

kcs.name: Modulo operator for divisibility testing
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use the % operator with 3, 5, and 15 to determine which FizzBuzz rule applies to each number.

kcs.name: Conditional priority ordering
kcs.reasoning: All solutions test divisibility by 15 first, then 3, then 5 to ensure correct precedence (FizzBuzz before Fizz or Buzz alone).

kcs.name: String conversion of integers
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use String.valueOf() to convert numbers to strings for the default case when no divisibility rule applies.

kcs.name: Array element assignment in loops
kcs.reasoning: Each iteration assigns a computed string value to the appropriate array index within the loop.

70.problem_description: Verify that every occurrence of the number 2 in an array is adjacent to at least one other 2 (no isolated 2s allowed).

kcs.name: Array boundary edge case handling
kcs.reasoning: Solutions must distinguish between first element, last element, and middle positions to safely access neighbors without index-out-of-bounds errors.

kcs.name: Adjacent element neighbor access
kcs.reasoning: All solutions check nums[i-1] and/or nums[i+1] to verify that any 2 found has at least one neighboring 2.

kcs.name: Nested conditional logic for case separation
kcs.reasoning: Multiple if-else chains partition logic based on array position and value, handling first/last/middle elements differently.

kcs.name: Early return pattern in loops
kcs.reasoning: Solutions immediately return false upon detecting an isolated 2, short-circuiting further iteration.

kcs.name: Boolean default case logic
kcs.reasoning: The method returns true as the default (all 2s properly paired) and only returns false when a violation is found.

kcs.name: Loop index with boundary checking
kcs.reasoning: Solutions use array length to determine safe iteration bounds and prevent accessing out-of-range indices.

71.problem_description: Determine whether an array can be split at some index such that the sum of elements before the index equals the sum of elements after the index.

kcs.name: Array traversal with index-based loops
kcs.reasoning: All solutions iterate through the array using for-loops with explicit index management to visit each element.

kcs.name: Two-accumulator pattern for sums
kcs.reasoning: Each solution maintains two separate running totals representing opposite sides of a potential balance point.

kcs.name: Accumulator variable initialization and update
kcs.reasoning: Solutions initialize sum variables to 0 and increment them as array elements are processed.

kcs.name: Equality comparison for balance detection
kcs.reasoning: All solutions test the condition leftSum == rightSum to identify when the array is balanced.

kcs.name: Boolean return with early exit
kcs.reasoning: Solutions return boolean and use early return statements when a balance point is found.

kcs.name: Array boundary management in loop conditions
kcs.reasoning: Solutions carefully set loop bounds to avoid comparing invalid partition points (e.g., checking before last element only).

kcs.name: Parity check optimization for filtering
kcs.reasoning: Solution 2 demonstrates early termination by checking if total sum is odd, since odd-summed arrays cannot be balanced.

112.problem_description: Generate an array containing a triangular series pattern where each iteration k contains the sequence 1 through k, repeated for iterations 1 to n.

kcs.name: Nested loop iteration patterns
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use nested loops where the outer loop runs n times and the inner loop runs i times, requiring understanding of how nested loops interact to generate specific patterns.

kcs.name: Triangular number array sizing
kcs.reasoning: All solutions correctly calculate array length using the formula n*(n+1)/2, demonstrating knowledge that the total elements is the nth triangular number.

kcs.name: Array index tracking across loops
kcs.reasoning: Each solution maintains a separate index variable (p, i, m, x, or count) that advances independently of loop counters to properly populate the flat array.

kcs.name: Loop counter increment mechanisms
kcs.reasoning: Solutions demonstrate different increment strategies (post-increment in loop header vs. manual increment in loop body), both achieving the same traversal pattern.

kcs.name: Populate array with computed values
kcs.reasoning: All solutions assign sequential integers to array elements based on nested loop logic, requiring proper use of array assignment syntax.

kcs.name: Method return of array reference
kcs.reasoning: The method returns an integer array reference after population, requiring correct non-void return type and array handling.

118.problem_description: Perform a left circular shift on an integer array, where each element moves one position left and the first element wraps to the end.

kcs.name: Circular array rotation algorithm
kcs.reasoning: All solutions implement left rotation by either in-place shifting or creating a new array with reordered elements.

kcs.name: Temporary variable for element preservation
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 1, 3, 5 save the first element in a temporary variable to avoid losing it during the shift operation.

kcs.name: Loop boundary management with length
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use careful loop bounds (typically i < nums.length - 1) to shift elements without causing index out-of-bounds errors.

kcs.name: Modular arithmetic for index wraparound
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 2 and 4 use modulo operator or conditional logic to map indices circularly when wrapping the first element.

kcs.name: Array length validation and edge cases
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 1, 3, 5 include conditional checks (length >= 2) to handle arrays too small to shift meaningfully.

kcs.name: Array instantiation and element copying
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 2 and 4 create new arrays and populate them by copying shifted elements, requiring understanding of array creation syntax.
